Title: Engineered coumarin accumulation reduces mycotoxin-induced oxidative stress and disease susceptibility

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Nicotiana benthamiana; Arabidopsis; Soybean; Tobacco BY-2 cells
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Nicotiana benthamiana; Arabidopsis thaliana; Glycine max; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient overexpression; stable overexpression; HPLC metabolite profiling; GC–MS confirmation; subcellular localization by GFP fusion/confocal microscopy; ROS assays; pathogen/nematode bioassays; cell viability assay
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms scopoletin, the precursor of scopolin; enables accumulation of coumarin pool
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates feruloyl-CoA at the 6' position to produce the key intermediate leading to scopoletin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as key/limiting enzyme for scopoletin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: branch-defining upstream committed step for scopoletin formation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Driven constitutively in transgenic leaves, roots, seeds and BY-2 cells; metabolite accumulation strongest in Arabidopsis roots and soybean shoots/leaves; leaf fluorescence strongest in veins and trichomes
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S promoter (constitutive) in transient assays; constitutive overexpression in stable transformants
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, roots and seeds; in leaves especially veins and trichomes; scopolin mainly intracellular, scopoletin enriched in culture medium/extracellular fraction
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mechanical tissue damage; Fusarium virguliforme infection; fusaric acid treatment; deoxynivalenol treatment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes constitutive accumulation of scopoletin and especially scopolin, reduces mycotoxin-induced ROS and cell death, decreases Arabidopsis susceptibility to Heterodera schachtii, and increases soybean tolerance to sudden death syndrome caused by Fusarium virguliforme.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Heterologous AtF6’H1 expression was sufficient to induce scopoletin accumulation in Nicotiana benthamiana, Arabidopsis, soybean and tobacco BY-2 cells. 2. In stable transformants, coumarins accumulated mainly as scopolin, with strong organ-dependent differences between Arabidopsis and soybean. 3. Mechanical damage rapidly converted scopolin to scopoletin, indicating a releasable defense-associated coumarin pool. 4. High root scopolin in Arabidopsis coincided with reduced Heterodera schachtii infection. 5. In soybean and BY-2 cells, AtF6’H1-mediated coumarin accumulation attenuated fusaric acid/deoxynivalenol-induced ROS and cell death, associating with improved tolerance to Fusarium virguliforme disease.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious growth or developmental penalty detected in Arabidopsis or soybean under tested conditions; direct antifungal activity against F. virguliforme appears limited at in planta metabolite levels.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper states F6’H1 is the exclusive limiting enzyme for mainly producing scopoletin from feruloyl-CoA. Low esculin accumulation suggests possible minor in vivo activity toward caffeoyl-CoA, but this was not a core focus. Scopoletin remained low while scopolin predominated, implying rapid glycosylation/storage after synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant coumarin associated with reduced oxidative stress, reduced mycotoxin-induced cell death, and enhanced tolerance/resilience to pathogens and pests.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for engineering coumarin enrichment to improve crop disease resilience, especially tolerance to Fusarium-associated oxidative damage. Likely useful in gene stacking with secretion/transport or glucosidase deployment strategies to enhance extracellular defense efficacy; caution that direct antifungal effects may require higher metabolite accumulation or targeted tissue/surface delivery.
